petitioner_background: The petitioner is a self-represented individual from China seeking classification as an alien of extraordinary ability in the martial arts (wushu) under section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act. The petition was filed on January 3, 2005 with the Vermont Service Center. The petitioner's background includes participation in wushu competitions in China during the 1990s and an alleged appointment with a cultural arts broadcasting company in Qingdao.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted nine items: (1) 'Honor Certificate' from the 'People's Republic of Liaoning Province' appraising petitioner as the 'activity Individual' of that province (1999); (2) 'Honor Certificate' from the 'Culture Bureau of Shenyang City' appraising petitioner as the 'activity Individual' of that city (1994); (3) 'Honor Certificate' stating petitioner 'won the Golden Award of Long Hui Sword Competition' at the ''Chao Sai De Cup' of the 3rd Annual International Wushu Communication Competition' (1997); (4) 'Honor Certificate' stating petitioner won the ''Yong Lin Cup' Ten Outstanding Wushu Award' (1990); (5) Fill-in-the-blank 'Certificate' stating petitioner 'achieved the fist [sic] of M (Middle Age) Group C Changquan' at the 99' China Qingdao International Wushu Championship (August 27, 1999); (6) Fill-in-the-blank certificate allegedly from the 'National Physical Education Sports Committee of the People's Republic of China' stating petitioner won the 'Fourth Grade Award in the Male Other Weapon Competition' at the 'National Wushu Competition' (1998); (7) Certificate naming petitioner a 'First Level National Outstanding Athlete of 1999'; (8) Undated fill-in-the-blank certificate from the 'Chinese Wushu Association' stating petitioner 'passed the Wushu Dan Examination'; (9) 'Honor Certificate' naming petitioner 'National Wushu Outstanding Individual' (1996).
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO found multiple deficiencies. First, the translations accompanying the award certificates were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3). Items 1 and 2 reflect only local or provincial recognition rather than national or international recognition. For items 3, 5, and 6, the petitioner provided no evidence of official comprehensive results for the competitions (event programs, participant lists, or rankings), which large-scale competitions typically issue. The AAO noted it was unusual that petitioner, age 29 at the 1999 competition, would compete in a 'Middle Age' category. For all nine items, there was no evidence of contemporaneous publicity surrounding the awards or evidence showing they command substantial recognition. Simply alleging an award is nationally recognized does not suffice; no documentation from the awarding entities or print media established that the awards are nationally or internationally recognized. Additionally, the record included no evidence of any awards received after the 1990s, suggesting petitioner's acclaim has not been sustained.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted what is alleged to be his membership certificate for the Chinese Wushu Association (CWA) indicating 'Dan: 7.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO explained that to meet this criterion, the petitioner must show the association requires outstanding achievement as an essential condition for admission, judged by recognized national or international experts, and that members must be selected at the national or international level rather than local or regional. The petitioner did not explain how attaining level 7 Dan demonstrates he is one of the small percentage who have risen to the very top of the field. The record did not include the membership bylaws or official admission requirements for the CWA. There was no evidence showing that admission to CWA membership required outstanding achievement or that the petitioner was evaluated by national or international experts in consideration of his admission.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted: (1) a certificate stating that his biographical sketch was included in 'A Dictionary of Contemporary Chinese Martial Artists'; (2) two articles allegedly published in the Liaoning Evening News and the Half Island City Newspaper in 2002; and (3) a document entitled 'Liaoning Evening newspaper' submitted in response to the RFE and again on appeal.
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: Published material must be primarily about the petitioner and printed in professional or major trade publications or other major media with significant national or international distribution. Regarding the dictionary, the record included no evidence of the actual published material about petitioner appearing in the publication, failing the plain wording requirement to submit the material with title, date, and author. The two 2002 newspaper articles were not accompanied by certified translations as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3), and there was no evidence these publications have substantial national readership or that petitioner has been subject of media coverage subsequent to 2002. The 'Liaoning Evening newspaper' document's source was not identified and lacked certified translation; further, its final two sentences discussed an unrelated farmers' paint drawings artist, which raised doubts about the accuracy of the translation. Under Matter of Ho, the petitioner must resolve inconsistencies with independent objective evidence. The AAO found no evidence that petitioner earned sustained acclaim in the national media of China or the United States.

evidence_per_criterion.evidence_submitted: The petitioner submitted a 'Letter of Appointment' dated March 5, 1996 allegedly issued by 'Ximen Cultural Art Broadcasting, Inc. of Quingdao' stating that it appointed the petitioner to be its 'director' and 'Wushu Movement Designer.'
evidence_per_criterion.aao_analysis: The AAO noted the document included no address, phone number, or other contact information for the company. It had not been shown that the organization has earned a distinguished reputation at the national or international level. The petitioner also did not submit evidence detailing the dates of his service, his specific responsibilities for the company, or his individual importance to the company's overall success. The AAO found the petitioner had not established that he performed in a leading or critical role for a distinguished organization, nor that his involvement earned him sustained national or international acclaim.

outcome_reasoning: The AAO dismissed the appeal, finding that the petitioner failed to demonstrate he meets at least three of the regulatory criteria necessary to establish sustained national or international acclaim. Review of the record did not establish that the petitioner distinguished himself to such an extent that he achieved sustained national or international acclaim or was within the small percentage at the very top of his field; the evidence was not persuasive that his achievements set him significantly above almost all others in his field at the national or international level. Beyond the director's decision, the AAO also found the petitioner failed to satisfy 8 C.F.R. § 204.5(h)(5), which requires clear evidence that the alien is coming to the United States to continue work in the area of expertise, as the record contained no such evidence. Additionally, although the petitioner indicated on the Form I-290B that a brief and/or evidence would be submitted within 30 days, more than two months had passed with nothing further received. Each ground was considered an independent and alternative basis for denial, and the burden of proof was not met.
legal_citations: Section 203(b)(1)(A) of the Immigration and Nationality Act, 8 U.S.C. § 1153(b)(1)(A)
Spencer Enterprises, Inc. v. United States, 229 F. Supp. 2d 1025 (E.D. Cal. 2001), aff'd 345 F.3d 683 (9th Cir. 2003)
fraud_or_procedural_issues: Multiple procedural issues were identified: (1) translations of foreign-language documents were not certified as required by 8 C.F.R. § 103.2(b)(3); (2) the translation of the 'Liaoning Evening newspaper' document contained two sentences about an unrelated farmers' paint drawings artist, raising doubts about the accuracy of the translation and invoking Matter of Ho regarding the petitioner's duty to resolve inconsistencies with independent objective evidence; (3) the AAO noted it was unusual that the petitioner, who would have been 29 at the time, competed in a 'Middle Age' category at the 1999 China Qingdao International Wushu Championship; (4) the 'Letter of Appointment' from Ximen Cultural Art Broadcasting lacked any contact information; and (5) the petitioner indicated on Form I-290B that a brief and/or evidence would be submitted within 30 days but failed to submit anything more than two months later.
